sentences.text: I was selected for an officer's candidate school.
They took over what they call the Ludovica Academy.
Ludovica Academy used to be the-- like a West Point of Hungary, you know, in a small scale.
And I was there in the-- you know, all kind of a training we had to go through.
First of all, we all had to get split-naked and make sure, you know, you're not circumcised, so you're not Jewish.
This was the first thing, you know?
That's the test you went through.

Second, you know, you have to go through a test that how you feel about the Third Reich, how you feel about the communism, how you feel about the Jews, would you follow order blindly if whatever order was given to you?
So naturally, I mean, I had to sign everything, OK.
Because that's the reason I wanted to get in to get the information.
I was there on a training.
And after I was trained, they put me into the headquarters, the division in Budapest.
The headquarters division in Budapest was in Andrassy Street.
That's a main street of Budapest.
And right across from the main-- where office was, was the Hungarian Nazi Party headquarters, on Andrassy ut 60.
That's what they-- what they called the Arrow Cross people, they were there.
You know, I had very little to do with the Arrow Cross people, as such, because I was assigned as a liaison between the Hungarian army and the SS operations in Budapest and the surrounding area.
I had the first-hand information of everything, naturally.
I was given full confidence, naturally.
In fact, they even gave me the Panzer Cross, you know, I never even been in a Panzer, because they liked me so much, you know?
So I just show you this, you know, what sometimes personality can do to-- to-- to achieve things.
And I-- giving me a-- a-- a-- what they call, a headquarters car, which was a Mercedes, with the two flags on the front, you know, with the German headquarters's flag and the regular German flag.
And I used this car.
That was my car to use whenever I go anywhere, whenever I, for every reason, I had to go.
At night, I went to Mr.
Schweikert's place, on Rakoczi street.
By this time, I had nothing to do with the man, my contact man, from Victory, because he disappeared off the scene.
Whatever happened to him, I never know, because my only contact was this Mr.
Schweikert.
And-- and then, I took every day home papers from the German headquarters.
And we typed it, in his office, as a safe conduct paper.
And I had, with me, the great seal of the German headquarters, and I stamped it.
And the commander, Wiedemayer, signed.
His name, I signed on it, on every one of them.
Now the paper was written in German purposely.
This is so the Hungarian Nazis could not question it, because it would have been Hungarian, they would have questioned it.
But since it was German, 90% of them could not-- 99% of them couldn't read it.
It was written in German.
And it said these-- these people, whose name was on it, regardless that he's wearing the Jewish cross-- yeah, I mean the Magen David, is a important part of the German headquarters.
He works for the German headquarters and, as such, should not be, molested and shouldn't be harmed, signed Wiedemayer.
And we made this paper out.
This was a safe conduct paper.
I left it with him every night.
I did-- did not distribute any of the papers.
I left with him, who he gave to the people, who he has to give that to, OK?
Then, this went on for all the way till the-- the summertime.
And then, when they started to build the ghetto in Hungary, and when they build a ghetto in Budapest, you see, by this time, all the people in the outlying areas, they were deported.
The only people who left, they were the people of Budapest, Jewish people.
They made the ghetto in Budapest.
And we had some friends, you know, who were Jewish, people who work in the newspapers and so on.
So I picked-- first-- the first person I picked up, it was my cousin, who was a Jewish guy, Dr.
Markovich.
And I took him into my aunt's factory.
Now, first time, we never thought of it to open the hole and put him in, you know, in the sewer system.
We just told him, you just hide underneath the benches and sleep there and everything what you can do, you know?
Then more and more people I picked up.
My aunt became panicky.
Because they have to feed these people.
You-- this wasn't just like leaving people right there, you know, and-- and-- and-- on their own.
They couldn't get out to buy food.
Besides, there was no food available, you know?
And-- and so what happened, we had to find a place for them to hide.
So we-- as an experiment, I said, let's see what's behind the wall.
What we thought, at the time, to be honestly, I thought that we didn't know this was a sewer in between.
We thought the wall only separated the two parts of her-- her factory, until we opened the wall.
And then we seen that this was actually a big hole there, where the sewer system was.
We said, that's perfect.
We can put the people right here.
And my aunt set up a little kitchen and start to feed, first, two people, first, three people, and more and more and more, as I brought them in, that-- that people.
Now, it wasn't so simple, because the-- Mr.
Schweikert told me who were the important people, who, he felt, they were in immediate danger of deportation, because of their former positions in politics, in literature, and so on.
So I-- I took those people.
And I had a free ride on the ghetto, because I had the German military car.
I had my-- always wear my helmet, because this looks very impressive, you know?
And I went in, and I took people out, you know, from the ghetto, with no questions.
Nobody asked questions.
You know, I always told them, I take them to the headquarters for questioning.
And instead of this, I took them to my aunt's place, you know.
Her husband was petrified again, you know.
He wouldn't even close to the place.
He just stayed to the home.
He was afraid if anybody find out, he will be the first one to be shot.
[LAUGHS] So he was a chicken, as like I said, you know?
And he had absolutely nothing to do with it.
Everything, all the credit goes to my aunt and my cousin, who helped, at a young girl, at the time, I believe was 12 years old, who helped cooking and everything with all the people.
Now, that's when this friend of mine told me this.
Why don't I go in, because his brother was one of the, what they call a camp-- pre-- what they called, an assemble camp, for the concentration camp, and try to get him out.

So I made-- in the headquarters, I made up a paper, which says this, you know, for myself, to cover myself, this I, George Nemeth, in-- the liaison officer for the German headquarters, and I ordered-- I-- by order of the headquarters, everybody should cooperate with me and follow my orders.
Wiedemayer signed it again, naturally, which was me.
[LAUGHS]
And with this paper, I went into the-- this assembly camp, you know.
And I told them, I'm looking for a person who has an information what we urgently needed, military information.
So they lined up a couple thousand people for me, in a row, so I can drive through with my car.
And a-- a-- a Hungarian-- a German officer was with me, in the car, you know, from the camp.
And they told me, let me know if you see the person, you know.
So finally, I find him.
So I pointed out, that's the one.
I stopped the car.
I told him.
He jumped out the car, and he took him.
And he-- he told him that you sit in the back.
At this point, he was with him, another guy, who was my schoolmate, also, like him.
And as he yelled out, he come out.
He said, take me, too.
Take me, too, with you.
Fortunately, the German officer didn't know what he was saying.
So I just went there, and I punched him out, you know, because this was the difference.
Not only I would have died, right there, but the only contact, what they had in the inside the SS, would have died with it.
I had no other choice, you know.
Like a-- a-- I did not kill him or anything like it, but I just had to eliminate him to come with me.
You know, I did not-- except, you know, punched him a couple times, and I drove off with him.
And-- but fortunately, you know, nothing happened, you know.
And the German officer asked me what happened.
I told him that's probably his brother.
He want to come with his brother, you know.
And he said, pah, just leave him alone.
And so I-- I took him out.
And then-- it happened October the 15th.
Now October the 14th, it was a Saturday evening.
I went to the headquarters of the underground, on Rakoczi Street.
And there was an English intelligence officer for the Secret Service there.
And he told Mr.
Schweikert that tomorrow, Sunday, October the 15th, Admiral Horthy, who was the head of the Hungarian government at the time, will make a speech at noontime on the Hungarian radio.
And he will announce that we are breaking up with the Germans.
And if you see from American or English planes coming and dropping parachuters, please welcome them, because they are our friends.
And don't fight the Germans.
Just let them leave the country.
He made the speech.
Actually, he made it, as history will tell you, if you check it out, he made this speech on a Hungarian radio, October the 15th, at noontime exactly.
And it was a beautiful day, like today.
I mean, there was no-- not a cloud in the sky.
And we we just looking, looking, and looking.
And the planes never came.
And nobody ever came.
The Germans was already going to Austria, to the German border.
They were ready to leave.
And when suddenly see that nothing happened, they all came back.
And they took-- went up to the king's palace, to where Admiral Horthy was, his residence.
They put-- rolled him in, into a Persian carpet, so people don't see it.
But first, they killed all his bodyguards, the palace guards.
And took him, Austria, to a-- a camp.
To a-- it wasn't a concentration camp, just what they call a safe camp, whatever they call it.
You know, when-- like average people put concentration camp.
They put in a comfortable place, where they can keep an eye on them.
And the Germans came back.
And next day, the Nazis took over completely.
The Arrow Cross people took over the country completely.
And start to-- and it cost 300,000 Jewish lives, because that's when all them, right away, started to deport all the Jews.
This-- and I find out, the following day, when I went up there to Mr.
Schweikert, and I asked him, what happened?
What did this happen?
He said, it was a mistake for to begin with.
The Secret Service planned this.
The military planned this.
But in the last minute, it was stopped by the so-called diplomats, because, in Yalta, Roosevelt, Stalin, and Churchill agreed this-- the Russians will occupy Hungary.
And they could not break this agreement.
That's the reason they did not send the paratroopers.
This was one day, October 15, 1944, which I never forget.
Now, I was still active part, naturally, whatever I had to do.
Nightly, I went up to his place, with the German commando car, and picked up American and English flyers at night, under the cover of the night.
And I took over to Csepel Island.
I gave it to the partisans, who took over.

And then, in turn, took whatever was necessary to take them to a, you know, either to France or England or, at the time, Italy was already-- there was American bases in Italy, to go back to their units, you know, and to save them.
That happened nights after nights after nights, you know.
Except, one night, when I ran into a very reluctant German military police group, and they stopped my car.
At the time, the cars had just a special slit, in the front, you know, because of the air raids.
They don't want, you know, the full light, just a small slit on the light.
They were all blacked out, the rest of them.
And I seen them.
They're stopping me, you know.
And-- they had the chain, you know, special military-- military police has a special chain, which says 'Military Police' on here.
And they asked me, what's going on?
So I told him, I'm taking these people to the headquarters.
So he said, what right do you have to take them to the headquarters?
And I took my paper, which I made up for myself, you know.
They were reading this paper, you know.
And as I looked at them, I see that one of the German soldiers, the military policeman, put the magazine into his machine gun.
So it wasn't [LAUGHS] too many questions to ask, you know.
So fortunately, I was maybe a second faster than they were.
And that was the only time, really, even-- that's one of the many times when my life was very much in danger.
The following time, it was a Saturday.
I cannot remember the date exactly.
This was in November.
I was-- I supposed to go to the headquarters, which was in the Hatvani Palace, in-- in-- right next to the King's Palace.
Hatvani was a-- a Hungarian, Jewish noblemen, who owned sugar factories.
And he had a tremendous, big palace, not far from the King's Palace.
They asked us to go there.
That's where-- where Eichmann was there.
That's where Otto Skorzeny was there.
Otto Skorzeny is, by the way, the man who freed Mussolini from Montegrosso, you know, with a small plane, the Folke-Wulf Storch was the name of the plane.
And there were all the Hungarian Nazis together there.
We were heading toward this direction, when-- this was Saturday afternoon, around 2 o'clock.
The bridge was full of people.
I mean that the bridge is almost a mile long, over the Danube River.
In the middle of the river is a island, Margaret Island.
And as I drive up on the-- I was driving, I believe, in a second section on the river, because there are pillars there.
Suddenly, I heard a tremendous explosion.
Oh, no, I'm wrong.
I'm ahead of myself.
As I drive on the-- on the bridge, I see two German soldiers jumping over the railing and yelling, frenetically, achtung, achtung!
Pass auf, pass auf, which means, watch out, watch out.
Something is wrong.
You know, you can tell.
And I hardly finished what they were saying, I heard an explosion in the-- and in the rear-view mirror-- I was driving a-- a little, what they, at the time, they called it a DKW, at the time.
Because my other car, overnight, was destroyed.
So I was driving, what they call, today, an Audi.
And this was an-- or a two-door Audi, which the door opened like in the front, not like the cars open this way.
You know, like almost when the mirror was, that's how we open the car.
And then in the mirror, I see, suddenly, the first part of the bridge lifting up like this.
You know?
And-- and-- and suddenly, in my mind, you know, it's-- it's-- it's everything in split second.
You know, you don't have time to think.
You know, that's your-- your animal instinct take over.
And I thought, my god, they are bombing the bridge.
Who would be?
I didn't hear no planes.
I thought maybe the Russians, with a long-range artillery, are bombing the bridge.
And this moment, I heard a second explosion.
And a second part of the bridge I see lifting up.
You know, and-- and they were full of street cars, and a German military convoy was also on the bridge the same time.
And this point, I thought, it's no joke anymore.
I stopped the car.
I shut the door.
And I started to run.
Because in the middle of the bridge, there was like an underpass, which led to the Margaret Island.
And I, in my mind, I thought, I'll try to reach this.
Maybe I can go to the island.
And as I run out, the next steps I tried to make, it was no place to step.
There was no place to step.
The bridge was gone.
And I went up to the bridge.
It's-- it's interesting, because I-- I-- I told my children this, you know.
You know what my last thought was when-- when I had no place to step or-- or no place to go?
I was thinking of, at least I will be with my mother.
It's a funny thing.
But this was my thought.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And when I came to, I don't know how long it took.
But a German, a little pontoon boat came, and since I was in a German uniform.
The rest of them were all floating away, everybody.
I was approximately about four feet.
The bridge came down.
Here was the pillar.
And this pillar came down and went to the river like this.
And I was by this part of it, but five feet or four feet from the water.
All around me was dead people.
The last thing I can remember was a horse-drawn carriage there.
And the horses were still kicking, and they kicking the people who were already dead.
But in that last kick, you know, this was my last memory there.
And the military, the German military pontoon picked me up and took me to the hospital.
I didn't have a scratch, not a scratch.
So somebody up there looked out for me, as simple as this.
And-- and-- and I, since then, I believe in this myth what my aunt told me.
My aunt told me this when my mother died, on her death bed, told her, you take care of my son.
And if any time he needs me or he's in trouble, just tell him to pray to me.
And I still believe in, today, this, that's my dead mother looking out for me.
And apparently, she did.
Then, I was taken to the hospital and checked out.
I was nothing wrong with me.
I went, and I checked with the people, you know, whom were in hiding.
Then, I had to go and pick up more people.
And the Russians came closer and closer.
And I had to go up and work with Otto Skorzeny.
Otto Skorzeny, he was in charge SS, like he was something like a SS brigadier general, you know, with--
His big thing was, he was driving a BMW 1,000, you know, with the side-car and a bazooka mounted this.
This was his-- his big thing, you know, this?
He-- he's invincible, you know, he felt, you know.
So he wanted to work with me.

He wanted me to go with him to Germany, you know, and to be a liaison between the Hungarian army, who were, at the time, since the Russians came closer and closer, they go-- they went to Austria, with them, you know, to go with them.
So I told him, sure.
I will do it.
Naturally, I wouldn't say no.
And-- and then, in-- we had to get food.
So I just went all kinds of places, you know, in German uniform.
And I just took whatever was there.
I went to restaurants, back room and everything.
And I had my machine gun with me, I think.
That's for the German army.
That's for the German army.
And then, when I went to the German army where they feed us, you know, in Ludovica Academy, I just took everything for the headquarters there, when I can get a hold to help my aunt feed the people.
Because you could not buy anything, any food, because there was no food.
And the money was worthless.
So my aunt, many times, went out to the outlying areas and gave all her jewelry, her rings, you name it, everything, in order to get food, in order to feed all these people.
By this time, there were at least, in my estimation, 60 people still left in that, you know, in the place.
Now feed 60 people just one meal a day, that's not easy, you know, to keep them alive.
And by the way, the people, every day, the men, every day, shaved in that place.
Every day, we-- I told them to shave in case, if anything happened, somebody-- something happen, they have to go on the street.
I don't want them to see them, you know, with beards and everything, to look like they are everyday people.
So they did shave, every day, the people under candle light or one little bulb, which was-- you seen it.
They were in there.
And that's what they did, you know, the people.
There were two pregnant women in the place, also.
You know, how they became pregnant, that's not of my business.
[LAUGHS] But they, nevertheless, they were-- when they came out, they was-- one of them, a couple of months later, had a baby, you know.
And my aunt and my cousin took care of all these people.
Her husband was nowhere to be seen, because he was-- he was just afraid of everything, you know, what-- what happened there.
And I worked with a man.
I know he was a Swede.
I never ask his name, because [LAUGHS] I-- it wasn't my business to asking anybody's name.
When you work with the intelligence, you don't introduce yourself, formally, like you go to a dance, you know?
Because, if-- if you get captured, and they torture you, you may come up with names, what you don't supposed to come out with.
So I worked with him.
And I gave him a lot of papers, what I made up, you know, safe conduct papers.
And in the-- like I showed you the tape, what the Hungarian government made in 1975.
I showed you this a little while ago.
In January the 8th, he came down.
The Russians came in, on the 8th, in the morning.
All the people-- by the afternoon, all the people left.
Some of them had to go for a couple hours to learn how to walk.
Because they did not-- couldn't walk.
They was just sitting there.
They came out, and they collapsed, because their legs could not hold them.
In there, he has to walk.
They also had to-- had to fear from the Russians, because the Russians shot everybody on sight.
Because, to them, everybody who was living was an enemy.
So it was-- they were in a double danger.
And this Swedish gentlemen came in, and because he know about the hiding place.
And he ask me, what happened to the people?
And I told him, we let them go.
Because, you know?
He said, that's fine.
You know, it's fine.
He congratulated my aunt, what a great job she did and all this thing, and tell me this, what I did, how-- what a great job I did.
And we walked out.
He said, let's go to the Russian headquarters.
We find out where it is.
He said, I-- I find out this was in the Royal Apollo Hotel, on a terrace, a [NON-ENGLISH], we call it.
That's one of the main thoroughfare in Budapest.
And on the way there, as we turned maybe a-- a block, you know, not even a block, a drunken Russian soldier stand there, with a Katyusha, a little machine gun.
And he told us, [RUSSIAN].
I, at the time, already shed the German uniform, naturally, even the shoes and everything, you know.
I put on everything, my own clothes.
So we go to this Russian soldier, who was obviously drunk as a skunk, because [LAUGHS] you just went close to him, you get drunk by being next to him.
And he said, [RUSSIAN], which mean to come here.
And he tried to herd us in to a yard, in an apartment house yard.
You know, there were, next door to an apartment houses, four, five, six, story apartment houses, each had a little inside yard.
And we tried to tell him, you know, that we are with the resistance movement.
We tried to show him the papers.
And that's when I find out that he was Wallenberg, because he said, I am Raoul Wallenberg.
I'm a Swedish diplomat.
And I wanted to go to the embassy.
And the soldier just took our papers and tore it in million pieces and put them in the snow, which was that high at the time, and herd us-- us in, into this yard.
